description: Table 1B dialog: Five Card Draw, frequency table, LLN, Surveillance Room. NPCs: Hank (returns), Lucky (gambler's fallacy). Gate: procedural relative frequency, conceptual Lucky rebuttal, transfer coin-flip LLN.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Table 1B. Same room, same game. But you're going to do something different here.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Every hand you play, I want you to track what you got and what happened. That running record — that's your frequency table. Watch it build.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Somebody new across from you today.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Word up. Don't let my record fool you — I'm about to go on a run. I keep getting trash cards — One Pair, High Card, the works. The deck has to deal me something good soon.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: We're still in the front room, so you still get to bet first on every hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: She says that every week. Watch that frequency table — and watch Lucky.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Keep tracking.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Note what you get.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Your table's building up.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Standing pat. Mark it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Starting fresh. Note it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good hand. Mark the win.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You took that one.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Lucky had it. Keep going.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Mark it. Keep tracking.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good fold.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Folded. Mark it.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Man. Look at these cards — One Pair again. I've been getting garbage for four hands straight. The deck has to give me something good soon. Law of averages.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Lucky thinks bad cards mean good cards are coming. That's not how a shuffle works.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Look at the hand types in your frequency table — not wins and losses. Is there any sign that getting One Pair a lot makes a better hand more likely next deal?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hold up. You've been tracking a while. See how some of those frequencies are starting to settle?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Ten hands is a start. I want to show you what happens when you run it at scale. The Nest has cameras everywhere — we can replay draws as fast as we want. Follow me.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Right in here. We're going to draw cards — one at a time, put it back, reshuffle — and track how often the card comes up red. You already know the answer: 26 red cards in a 52-card deck. Watch what happens when we do it ten thousand times.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You see that? The more draws you run, the closer the frequency gets to 26 out of 52. That's the Law of Large Numbers — not that you're 'due,' but that the long run converges on the real number.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You've tracked enough hands. Let's check what you're seeing.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Quick one. Say you tracked 50 hands and pairs showed up 20 times. What's the relative frequency of pairs as a percentage? Enter a whole number.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: 40%. 20 out of 50 — that's 0.40, or 40%. Your frequency table works the same way: count divided by total.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: A bit high. Relative frequency is count ÷ total. What's 20 ÷ 50?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's low. Count divided by total, then multiply by 100. What does 20 ÷ 50 × 100 give you?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: It's 40%. 20 divided by 50 is 0.40, which is 40%.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Now — Lucky. She keeps getting low-ranking cards and says the deck owes her a good hand. Your frequency table says otherwise. What specifically?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Lucky says 'Four bad hands in a row — the deck has to deal me something good soon.' What does your frequency table show that contradicts her? Check all that apply.

options.text: The hand type frequencies are consistent — getting One Pair often doesn't make a better hand more likely next deal.
Since Lucky has been getting bad cards recently, the deck is going to compensate with better ones soon.
Each hand is a fresh deal. The deck doesn't have a debt column.
The frequency table shows steady hand type rates — no spike toward better hands after a run of bad ones.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Right. Hand type frequencies are steady — no catch-up. Every deal is independent. Lucky's wrong — and your table proves it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One of those is exactly what Lucky believes. Which ones does the frequency table actually back up?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. Focus on what the data in the table actually shows.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The frequency table shows steady hand type rates — no sign that bad cards predict better cards coming. Each deal is independent. The deck doesn't track debts.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One more. In the Surveillance Room you watched red card frequency converge over ten thousand draws. That graph was showing you something specific.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The Surveillance Room showed the running frequency of red cards over 10,000 draws — one card at a time, put back and reshuffled each time. Choose everything that's true.

options.text: As more cards were drawn, the running frequency of red moved closer to 50%.
After 7 black cards in a row, the next draw is more likely to be red to balance things out.
Any single draw is still 26 out of 52 — the graph doesn't change the odds of one card.
The graph shows that long-run relative frequency converges toward the theoretical probability.
Since the frequency converged, the next 100 draws must include close to 50 red cards to stay on track.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's the Law of Large Numbers. Long-run convergence — not a prediction about any single draw. The deck doesn't owe Lucky a red card because the last seven were black.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One of those is the gambler's fallacy with a different face. The graph shows long-run behavior — not what any single draw will be.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. The graph shows the long run. Each individual draw is still 26 out of 52.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Running frequency converges over many trials — that's the long run. But each single draw is still 26 out of 52, and the convergence doesn't create a debt for any specific outcome.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You can read frequency data and you know what it does and doesn't say. That's the skill.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Lucky still thinks the deck owes her better cards. You know why it doesn't — and you've got the table to prove it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The Front Room is done. The Main Room is where things get more precise.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: I'm in.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Bet.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Check.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Good.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: One.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Two.

nodes.speaker: lucky
nodes.text: Three.
